coredata.dc:title: Data-driven system identification, model transfer, and forecasting of battery capacity with impedance measurements
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chenyi
Zhang, Long
coredata.dc:description: Accurate estimation and forecasting of lithium-ion battery capacity are critical challenges that can limit their application and reliability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) offers a non-invasive and information-rich means for capturing battery internal states, but existing EIS-based features, such as single-frequency features, equivalent circuit model (ECM) features, and machine-learning-extracted features, often suffer from issues of robustness, dependence on accurate ECM modeling, and interpretability. This paper proposes an efficient and transparent data-driven system identification approach using the Zero-Pole-Gain (ZPG) model to analyze EIS data, and uses the ZPG model pole as a feature for battery capacity estimation and multi-cycle forecasting with robustness to noise, battery, and temperature variation, and physical interpretability. Experimental validation using a dataset of 12 commercial LiCoO2/graphite cells across three temperatures demonstrates that the proposed pole feature outperforms three widely used features when evaluated using Polynomial, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) models, in terms of capacity estimation accuracy and robustness to noise and battery difference. The capacity estimation Root Mean Squared Percentage Error (RMSPE) of the proposed feature can be low up to 1.53% on the GPR model. Comprehensive results demonstrate the value of the proposed method as a promising alternative to conventional EIS feature extraction, with interpretability and considerable predictive performance, and without requiring prior battery knowledge.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Battery capacity estimation

head.ce:title: Data-driven system identification, model transfer, and forecasting of battery capacity with impedance measurements

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Oxford Road, M13 9PL, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Oxford Road, M13 9PL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate estimation and forecasting of lithium-ion battery capacity are critical challenges that can limit their application and reliability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) offers a non-invasive and information-rich means for capturing battery internal states, but existing EIS-based features, such as single-frequency features, equivalent circuit model (ECM) features, and machine-learning-extracted features, often suffer from issues of robustness, dependence on accurate ECM modeling, and interpretability. This paper proposes an efficient and transparent data-driven system identification approach using the Zero-Pole-Gain (ZPG) model to analyze EIS data, and uses the ZPG model pole as a feature for battery capacity estimation and multi-cycle forecasting with robustness to noise, battery, and temperature variation, and physical interpretability. Experimental validation using a dataset of 12 commercial LiCoO2/graphite cells across three temperatures demonstrates that the proposed pole feature outperforms three widely used features when evaluated using Polynomial, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) models, in terms of capacity estimation accuracy and robustness to noise and battery difference. The capacity estimation Root Mean Squared Percentage Error (RMSPE) of the proposed feature can be low up to 1.53% on the GPR model. Comprehensive results demonstrate the value of the proposed method as a promising alternative to conventional EIS feature extraction, with interpretability and considerable predictive performance, and without requiring prior battery knowledge.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium–ion batteries, renowned for their high energy density, high power density, and long lifespan [1], have found widespread applications in various fields such as portable and wearable electronic devices, sensors, and remote control devices, permeating almost every corner of our daily lives [2]. Precise capacity estimation is crucial for ensuring battery reliability and predicting the remaining lifespan [3]. For instance, in portable medical devices or wireless sensors, accurate capacity estimation and forecasting enable reliable working and timely maintenance and replacement. However, precise estimation and forecasting of battery capacity remain a significant challenge due to the complexity of battery capacity degradation [4].

For battery capacity estimation, Coulomb counting method is a common method which estimates battery capacity by measuring the integral of the current during the charging and discharging processes, see [5–7]. This method is simple and easy to implement but requires measurements over an entire charge–discharge cycle, which is often time-consuming [8]. Besides, Coulomb counting demands high-precision measurement equipment to reduce the accumulation of errors in the measuring process [4]. The Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) method is also widely used, which analyzes battery degradation based on the differentiation of the battery capacity over the battery voltage, such as research [9–11]. However, the ICA method may be sensitive to noise, especially at low charge–discharge rates [4,12]. Besides, the ICA method also needs long-term measurement, which may make it difficult to implement for real-time applications [8,13]. Overall, these methods have succeeded in some aspects, but fast and effective capacity estimation is still highly desirable.

In recent years, using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) for battery capacity estimation has gained widespread attention [4]. EIS is a real-time and non-invasive technique that analyzes the battery impedance response across various frequencies and reveals the electrochemical dynamics in the battery, providing deep insights into internal battery processes, such as state of charge, health conditions, and degradation mechanisms [14]. However, EIS contains frequency responses at multiple frequencies and has high data dimensionality as a result, and using EIS for capacity estimation often requires extracting appropriate features from EIS. In most of the existing research, the feature extraction method can be divided into three groups, including single-frequency-based features, equivalent circuit model (ECM) based features, and features extracted automatically by machine learning methods. The overview of these three types of feature extraction methods is provided below.

Firstly, some research employs EIS information at some specific frequencies for capacity estimation. For example, study [15] uses the variation analysis to identify the real part of the impedance (
Z
Re
) at 316 Hz as a reliable indicator of battery capacity. Their findings show that this frequency exhibits minimal variation with state-of-charge (SOC) under normal conditions, but significantly changes during battery degradation, making it suitable for capacity estimation. In study [16], a Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS) is used to excite the battery to rapidly obtain EIS data across a frequency range of 4 Hz to 1600 Hz. Based on these fast-acquired EIS measurements and a statistical clustering method,
Z
Re
at 88.8 Hz is found to vary significantly with state-of-health (SOH) while remaining stable across different SOC levels and excitation amplitudes. Thus,
Z
Re
at 88.8 Hz is employed as a feature and achieves considerable capacity estimation accuracy. Study [17] analyzes the low-frequency inflection points and the zero-crossing of the Nyquist plot and proposes a new feature—the ratio of
Z
Re
at 0.99 Hz and 1900 Hz—for capacity estimation. The above analysis shows that different studies employ various methodologies and draw differing conclusions regarding the importance of specific EIS frequencies. While this variation may partly arise from differences in battery types and experimental conditions, some researchers have selected entirely different frequency features even when working with the same dataset. For instance, using the dataset from [18], the authors themselves apply the Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD) method and identify the imaginary part of the impedance response (
Z
Im
) at 17.8 Hz and 233.87 Hz as the most effective features for capacity estimation for batteries cycled under 25 °C, and 17.8 Hz only for 35 °C and 45 °C batteries. In contrast, study [19], which also uses the same dataset, employed correlation and feature consistency analysis and found that
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz is the most important feature for capacity estimation. Overall, different studies have selected different frequency features from EIS data for capacity estimation, which significantly reduce data dimensionality, simplify the analysis, and often achieve good estimation performance. However, as EIS contains information of multiple frequencies, the selection of feature frequencies remains highly uncertain. Although each study provides a reasonable feature selection process, the chosen frequencies can differ by an order of magnitude or even more. Even when using cells from the same batch, the selection of characteristic frequencies may vary depending on which specific cells are used and which temperature the batteries are cycled at, due to cell-to-cell variations and the temperature effect on EIS. For instance, an EIS feature at a certain frequency may yield accurate capacity estimation for one cell and a specific temperature, but perform poorly on another temperature or another cell of the same type. As a result, single-frequency features may not sufficiently account for these variations and may lack robustness.

Besides, some studies use ECM to extract features from EIS for capacity estimation. ECM models battery dynamic behavior using circuit elements like resistors, capacitors, constant phase element (CPE), and voltage sources, and each component in the ECM has a clear physical meaning, making it easier to understand and correlate with battery degradation mechanisms [20]. For example, study [21] proposed a second-order Resistance-CPE (R-CPE) ECM, which enhances the traditional second-order Resistor–Capacitor (2-RC) model by incorporating two R-CPE units and temperature-dependent resistances to better fit EIS data and capture non-ideal electrochemical behaviors. From the fitted ECM, eight physical parameters (including the anode and cathode resistance, anode and cathode CPE capacitance, etc.) are extracted as features. These parameters are then used as inputs to a multi-output Gaussian process regressor to predict the battery capacity. Study [22] proposed an improved equivalent circuit model (ECMC) by modifying a standard second-order ECM with an additional capacitor parallel to the Warburg element to better capture low-frequency EIS behavior associated with electrode processes. From the fitted ECMC, the ECM parameters such as charge transfer resistance and capacitance, Warburg coefficient, CPE parameters, etc., were extracted as features for capacity estimation, achieving an average RMSE of 1.77%. Study [23] proposed a fractional-order impedance model to fit EIS data, integrating it with short-term relaxation effects to estimate battery capacity. The model includes a resistor, an inductor, two Zarc elements (a resistance element parallel with a CPE), and a diffusion-related CPE component. The sum of the resistance for the two Zarc elements was used as a feature for capacity estimation. Study [24] also proposed an ECM with two Zarc elements and a Warburg component to fit EIS data under varying conditions, and analyzed how the ECM model parameters vary with battery aging. Study [19] fit EIS data using a 2-RC ECM with CPE, and employ the CPE coefficient only as a feature for battery capacity estimation. Study [25] proposed a SOH-coupled nonlinear electro-thermal-aging model that integrates a 2-RC equivalent circuit model with temperature and capacity fade dynamics. From the model, ohmic resistance, charge transfer resistance, capacitance, and capacity are treated as SOH-dependent. These parameters are estimated in real-time using an extended Kalman filter, which simultaneously predicts SOC, SOH, and internal resistance. Study [26] proposed a temperature-SOC-aware capacity estimation method based on an ECM fitted to EIS data. The model consists of an inductor, ohmic resistance, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance, charge transfer resistance, and two CPEs. From the fitted model, charge transfer resistance is extracted as the key feature for capacity estimation. A probabilistic regression model is then constructed using charge transfer resistance, ambient temperature, and SOC as inputs to estimate battery capacity. Using ECM parameters fitted from EIS data as features offers several advantages. First, these parameters are physically interpretable, as each corresponds to specific electrochemical processes such as charge transfer resistance or SEI layer behavior, providing clear insight into degradation mechanisms. In addition, ECM features contain rich information—typically, only 6 to 10 parameters are needed to represent the full impedance spectrum. Lastly, compared to single-frequency impedance values, the fitted parameters are inherently more robust to disturbances caused by temperature or load fluctuations, since they are extracted from the entire EIS data. However, ECM design often requires prior battery knowledge, and a single battery may correspond to multiple possible ECM structures, making it difficult to determine which ECM is appropriate. For example, in study [22], six different ECM models were proposed to fit the EIS data, and their performance and fitting accuracy were analyzed in detail. Moreover, although some ECMs achieve good fitting results, the behavior of their circuit elements may not align with the actual physical processes in the battery. For instance, battery ohmic resistance typically increases with aging due to electrolyte degradation and reduced ionic conductivity. However, in the ECM proposed in study [22], the ohmic resistance was observed to decrease during battery aging and was therefore excluded as a feature for capacity estimation. Although the authors attributed this to the characteristics of the EIS data, it may also be due to a mismatch between the proposed ECM and the real battery, indicating that even well-fitted ECMs may lack physical consistency, posing challenges to the reliability of ECM-based feature selection and modeling.

Additionally, there are also some machine-learning-based methods that automatically extract informative features from EIS data for battery capacity estimation. For example, research [27] proposed a deep neural network with transfer learning to estimate battery SOH from EIS measurements collected at different temperatures. Research [28] proposed a hybrid approach for SOH estimation using EIS data, which uses a domain-adversarial neural network to estimate capacity and enhance transferability across datasets. Research [19] proposed a set of computation-light machine learning models for capacity estimation using EIS data, including both manually selected features such as
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz and CPE coefficient, and features automatically extracted via a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). Among them, the CNN-based approach achieves the best performance in accuracy, robustness to noise, and running speed. However, despite their strong predictive performance, machine-learning-based methods are essentially black-box models, and the internal mechanisms of the learned features often lack physical interpretability. In recent years, some studies have also employed interpretable machine learning methods to extract features from EIS data for battery capacity estimation. For example, research [29] proposed a deep learning framework for capacity estimation using EIS data, where the imaginary part of impedance across 60 frequencies is used as input to a one-dimension CNN with a self-attention mechanism. The self-attention mechanism is used to assign importance weights to different frequency components, highlighting which parts of the input contribute most to the capacity estimation. For example, research [30] proposed an interpretable capacity estimation framework that combines statistical features extracted from high- and low-frequency EIS components with temporal features derived from cycle-based sliding windows. SHapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) is employed to interpret the model outputs by quantifying the contribution of each feature. Similarly, research [31] proposed a SHAP-based interpretable machine learning framework for capacity estimation using EIS data, where the real and imaginary parts of impedance at 60 frequencies are used as input features. The SHAP values are computed to identify and rank the most informative impedance features, enhancing the interpretability. However, although attention mechanisms and SHAP-based machine learning methods can provide interpretability by analyzing the contribution of each input feature, such interpretation remains at the model output level rather than reflecting the underlying physical processes. For instance, machine learning methods may identify the
Z
Im
and
Z
Re
at certain frequencies as important features, but they cannot explain why these particular features are selected or what the combination of these key frequency features signifies. In other words, the features selected by machine learning lack the clear physical meaning of features such as ECM parameters or specific frequency points, making it difficult to reveal the evolution of battery degradation mechanisms.
Overall, the existing methods of battery capacity estimation and forecasting methods based on EIS data present three primary challenges:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Single-frequency-based features can significantly reduce data dimension and achieve good capacity estimation performance, but they may be sensitive to cell-to-cell variations and temperature differences. A feature at a specific frequency that performs well on a specific battery may not be accurate on another battery or under temperature variation conditions, limiting the generalization and robustness of single-frequency-based approaches.

ECM features are physically interpretable and compact, but building an accurate ECM often relies on prior battery knowledge. Besides, in some cases, even well-fitted models exhibit physically inconsistent trends, such as decreasing ohmic resistance during aging (as discussed above for research [22]). Selecting a reasonable ECM should consider not only the fitting accuracy, but also the physical meaning of ECM components based on the internal knowledge of batteries, limiting their application.

Machine-learning-based features, particularly deep learning, can achieve high accuracy in battery capacity estimation, but these models are black-box in nature, and the learned features often lack interpretability. Even with interpretable machine learning frameworks such as attention mechanisms or SHAP analysis, the interpretability of the identified features is limited to the input–output level and is often difficult to physically interpret battery internal electrochemical processes and degradation mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Upon the review of these studies, it is necessary to employ a transparent and easy-to-implement model for EIS modeling and extract robust and explainable features from EIS. Besides, feature selection should be less related to battery differences and temperature variations. To address the research gap mentioned above, this paper proposes a data-driven system identification (SID) technique for battery EIS modeling, representing EIS data with a Zero–Pole–Gain (ZPG) model. This approach treats the battery impedance as a dynamic system, where the EIS measurement represents the frequency response of that system. The ZPG model fitted from the EIS data can be regarded as the system’s transfer function. Since the ZPG model contains multiple zeros and poles, battery aging can be analyzed from the perspective of the physical meaning of poles and zeros in dynamic systems. For example, battery aging is commonly associated with increasing charge transfer resistance, which leads to slower dynamic responses in the EIS spectrum. In system identification theory, poles are directly related to the system’s dynamic behavior and time constants. Thus, as aging progresses and internal electrochemical processes become slower, the dominant pole tends to shift toward lower frequencies, demonstrating the potential of using these zeros and poles as features for capacity estimation. One of the poles of the ZPG model is selected as a feature for both capacity estimation and forecasting.
The key contributions of this research are summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The pole feature, which is also the pole of the impedance system, inherently captures battery dynamics and EIS over a range of frequencies compared to single-frequency-based EIS features. The selected feature does not vary with feature selection method and battery differences. Besides, since the pole corresponds to EIS at multiple frequencies and has an average effect in nature, it is less sensitive to frequency-level EIS disturbance due to temperature and battery variation, providing more robust capacity estimation. On a battery group that contains batteries cycled under 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C, the proposed feature outperforms three typical existing features with the lowest mean and standard deviation of Root Mean Squared Percentage Error (RMSPE) across different batteries, demonstrating the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed feature.

The pole feature is obtained directly from the ZPG model identified from EIS data, without assuming any predefined ECM. In addition, the ZPG model is a fixed mathematical structure that does not require any prior knowledge about the battery. Therefore, it can be used to fit EIS data from different types of batteries without the need to carefully design the ECM based on characteristics of the specific battery type.

The pole of a dynamic system is mathematically and physically interpretable compared to machine learning or deep learning features, as it reflects the system time constant or response speed around the pole frequency, which can be related to electrochemical phenomena. In addition, for the RC parallel circuit, which is commonly used in the ECM model, its transfer function is inherently a ZPG model format, and the pole of the system is −1/
R C
. Therefore, the system pole variation can also be interpreted as the change of RC. This is explained in more detail in Section 3.3.

ce:section.ce:para: In the following parts of this paper, the effectiveness of the proposed pole feature in capacity estimation and time series model capacity forecasting, as well as its noise robustness under noisy conditions, is verified. Besides, the proposed feature is also compared with three existing features in state-of-the-art research and demonstrates capacity estimation accuracy and robustness improvements. The entire process of this paper is illustrated in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: System identification methods for battery impedance modeling
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is information about battery internal impedance acquired by measuring the current response under voltage excitation across a wide frequency range. In this process, the battery impedance can be considered as a system input–output relationship, with voltage serving as input and current as output, and the EIS is the system frequency response. The SID method aims to identify the battery system by building a mathematical model using the EIS measurements. In this paper, the ZPG model is employed for SID, which can be represented as follows:

is the j th pole, m is the number of zeros, n is the number of poles. By modeling the battery impedance system via the ZPG model, the complex EIS information is compressed into a few system zeros and poles, which significantly reduces the data dimension. Notably, the SID approach in this research is data-driven, which relies on the EIS data, without requiring any prior knowledge about battery internal chemistry or physical characteristics.

The SID process, which is also the ZPG model training process, fits the ZPG model frequency response to the EIS data by optimizing the model parameters (zeros, poles, and gain). The SID process employs the nonlinear least-squares method, which minimizes the squared error between the model frequency response and the actual EIS measurements [32]. The error is defined as:

This non-linear least-squared problem is solved using the Sanathanan–Koerner iteration method [33] and the Vector Fitting method [34,35], which estimate the transfer function that best fits the EIS frequency response to reflect the battery impedance dynamics accurately. Through the SID approach, the rich and complex EIS information is distilled into a concise set of zeros and poles, representing the battery impedance system with only a few zeros and poles.

The EIS data, which encompasses details about the battery capacity, is compressed into zero-pole information. This allows using the ZPG model zeros and poles as features for battery capacity estimation. This study aims to use the smallest number of zeros and poles as a feature, and the feature selection and the capacity estimation process are introduced in this section.

First, the Spearman correlation coefficient (SCC) between capacity and different features is calculated to analyze feature importance. The reason for selecting the SCC to determine the most important feature is that a monotonic relationship is observed between most of the zeros and poles with battery capacity. SCC effectively evaluates the monotonic correlation between two variables and is defined as:
(3)

where

denotes the i th difference between the ranks of the corresponding variables, and n represents the number of observations. The range of SCC is −1 to 1. The SCC value of 1 and −1 indicates perfect positive and negative correlation, respectively. The SCC value of 0 implies that there is no monotonic correlation. Features with high absolute value of SCC and consistent across different batteries can serve as potential features for capacity estimation. Two potential features are identified through the above SCC analysis, and an ablation study, which involves using both features solely and their combination, is conducted in Section 3.4 to further identify the most effective feature.

After determining the most effective feature, this study validates the effectiveness of the proposed feature on three typical regression models, including Polynomial, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) models, and compares it with some typical features in existing studies. The Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) method, where each battery is iteratively used as the test set while the others serve as the training set, is used to comprehensively evaluate the feature effectiveness. The capacity estimation result is evaluated using the RMSPE criteria:

The entire process of feature selection and capacity estimation is summarized in Fig. 2.

In practical applications, historical battery capacity data is sometimes recorded, and battery capacity can be considered time series data, with each battery cycle considered a time step. In this case, using time series models such as the Auto-Regressive with eXogenous input (ARX) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models, the battery capacity can be forecasted, which is crucial for predictive maintenance and replacement planning. Thus, this section introduces the validation of the effectiveness of the proposed pole feature in time-series model forecasting.

Fig. 3 demonstrates the k-step ahead forecasting technique for time series models, where y is battery capacity, u is the external model input, t is the time step, k is the forecasting ahead step, and the predictor is a time series model. In Fig. 3(a), which represents the no-input condition, the model uses the historical capacity from time 0 to t to generate a predicted capacity after k steps,
y
ˆ
( t + k )
. Detaily, the model first uses the historical capacity to generate a forecasted capacity after one step,
y
ˆ

( 0 )

, and so on to
y
ˆ

. Besides, when EIS measurement is available, features from EIS can be used as external model input, as shown in Fig. 3(b). In this study, the pole feature is employed as the external input and compared with the no-input conditions to validate the performance improvement of adding the pole feature to the time series model. Two typical time series models are tested, which are the ARX model and the LSTM model, respectively, and the forecasting performance is also evaluated using RMSPE.

The data used in this study is from the open-source dataset generated by research [18]. The dataset employs 12 commercially available 45mAh LiCoO
2
/graphite Eunicell LR2032 lithium–ion batteries, which are widely used in applications such as wearable and portable devices, small medical equipment, and wireless sensors [18]. The batteries are tested in climate chambers at three different temperatures: eight batteries cycled at °C (marked as 25C01-25C08), and two batteries each at 35 °C and 45 °C (marked as 35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02, respectively). Each cycle consisted of constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging at a 1C rate (45 mA) to 4.2 V, followed by constant current discharge at a 2C rate (90 mA) to 3 V.

As for the EIS measurement, over 20,000 EIS spectra of these batteries are collected under various charge stages, capacities, and temperatures, making it the largest known dataset of its kind to date [18]. In the dataset, not every cycle involves measuring battery capacity and EIS. The capacity is measured at each odd cycle, while EIS is measured during nine different charging, discharging, and idle stages of each even cycle, with a frequency range from 0.02 Hz to 20 kHz and an excitation current of 5 mA. Since EIS measurements can be influenced during the battery charging and discharging stages and EIS measurements may disrupt normal battery operations during the charging and discharging process, EIS data collected at the idle stage (marked as Stage V in the dataset), which represents batteries after charging and resting for 15 min and remains relatively stable, are used in this study.

To identify the ZPG models of the battery system using the EIS data, proper model order selection is performed, where the ‘order’ refers to the number of zeros and poles. For instance, a sixth-order ZPG model includes six zeros and six poles. ZPG models of orders 4, 6, 8, and 10 are used to model the impedance system of the initial cycle of battery 45C02, and their frequency responses are compared with EIS measurements, as shown in Fig. 4. The fourth-order model shows notable discrepancies with EIS measurements while increasing the order to six significantly improved alignments. Beyond the sixth order, further improvements are minimal. Balancing accuracy and complexity, the sixth-order model is selected in this research.

To intuitively demonstrate the SID process, the ZPG model for the first cycle of battery 45C02 is derived in (5) as an example. The zeros and poles are extracted from the identified ZPG model and summarized with their corresponding corner frequencies in Table 1. The six zeros and six poles are arranged in ascending order based on their absolute values of angular frequency (in radians per second) and designated as ‘first’ to ‘sixth’. For instance, the ‘fourth pole’ refers to the pole with the fourth smallest angular frequency among all poles. Additionally, to simplfy the representation in the following research, each zero and pole is denoted as Zero n or Pole n , where n is the order of the zero or pole (e.g., the fourth pole is denoted as Pole4).

The zeros and poles, along with their associated corner frequencies converted from angular frequencies are depicted on the Bode plot in Fig. 5. The figure indicates that the zeros and poles distribute across the entire experimental frequency range, with each zero and pole covering a specific frequency range. The frequency response resulting from these individual zeros and poles collectively forms the overall frequency response of the identified system. Notably, the EIS measurement ranges from 0.02 Hz to 20 kHz, but the first zero and pole are outside this range. In Fig. 5, the identified frequency range of the identified system (red line) is expanded to illustrate the position of the first zero and pole.

In addition to the first cycle of battery 45C02, the SID method for EIS modeling is also reliable across various life stages of different batteries. For example, the EIS of batteries at three different temperatures, 25C08, 35C02, and 45C02, are modeled at SOH levels of 100%, 95%, 90%, 85%, and 80%, as shown in Fig. 6, and the model frequency responses are compared with EIS measurements. The ZPG model frequency responses demonstrate remarkable consistency with EIS measurements, indicating that the SID method performs reliably across different batteries and SOH levels, assuring its suitability for battery capacity estimation and forecasting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical explanation of zeros and poles
ce:section.ce:para:

The two poles are

, indicating that each RC parallel module provides a pole. Their associated frequencies are

, respectively. Through the pole frequencies, the two poles of the 2-RC ECM can be physically explained [20]: one is associated with the diffusion process, typically in the frequency range below 0.1 Hz, and the other is for the charge transfer process, typically in the 0.1 Hz–1000 Hz range.

Similarly, a sixth-order ZPG model corresponds to a 6-RC ECM, as illustrated in Fig. 8. Each parallel RC module corresponds to a pole in the ZPG model. According to Table 1, these poles cover frequencies approximately at 0.01 Hz, 0.1 Hz, 1 Hz, 40 Hz, 180 Hz, and 1600 Hz. The first two poles (below 0.1 Hz) represent diffusion processes, the third to fifth (from 0.1 Hz to 1000 Hz) correspond to charge transfer, and the sixth reflects ohmic resistance [20]. Compared to the 2-RC ECM, the sixth-order ZPG model has more poles, enabling broader frequency coverage and resolution. Although some studies have adopted more complex 3-RC or 4-RC ECMs, incorporating ideal elements such as CPE to better fit the impedance response and offer a more detailed representation of battery behavior, designing such ECMs requires prior knowledge of the battery, and a single battery may correspond to multiple possible ECM structures, which makes it difficult to determine which ECM is more reasonable. Moreover, the physical validity of each added component remains subject to verification, which also poses a challenge for the ECM-based approach. In contrast, for the ZPG model via SID method, it is fundamentally data-driven and does not require prior knowledge of internal battery mechanisms, although the ZPG model with order n can be interpreted through n-RC ECM. In a dynamic system, the system pole inherently corresponds to the system behavior around the pole frequency. In the application of batteries, the pole frequencies can be linked to the frequency of battery internal electrochemical processes, thus reflecting battery dynamics and offering a certain level of interpretability. This is further discussed in the next section.

In the subsequent analysis, Pole4 is selected as a feature for capacity estimation and is observed to exhibit a strong negative relationship with capacity, which is discussed in the next section. This observation aligns with the physical processes during battery aging. Firstly, the negative relationship indicates that the value of Pole4,
− 1 /
R
4
C
4
, increases with battery degradation, and the increase of
− 1 /
R
4
C
4
implies the increase of
R
4
C
4
. Since Pole4 corresponds to the frequency of approximately 40 Hz (typically associated with the charge transfer process [20]) and R primarily influences the value of
R C
[36], the increase of
R
4
C
4
implies an increase in charge transfer resistance, which aligns with actual changes within the battery and confirms the physical interpretability of the pole feature. Moreover, poles also correspond to the time constants of dynamic systems. For a typical RC module, the time constant is given by
τ = R C
. Therefore, an increase in the RC product also implies an increase in the time constant. For the charge transfer process corresponding to Pole4, a longer time constant suggests a slower electrochemical response, indicating degraded system kinetics and reduced efficiency in charge transfer [37]. This is also consistent with the actual battery degradation process, which further demonstrates the physical meaning of the Pole4 feature.

The ZPG model, identified from EIS, captures EIS variations during battery aging. Thus, ZPG model zeros and poles can be potential features for capacity estimation. However, a ZPG model includes several zeros and poles. In this section, the importance of these zeros and poles is evaluated through SCC and ablation testing.

Firstly, the SCC between different zeros and poles with capacity is calculated across all 12 batteries, as shown in Fig. 9(a), where each row represents a battery, and each column represents a feature. The SCC ranges from −1 to 1, where 1 indicates a perfect positive monotonic relationship, −1 indicates an ideal negative monotonic relationship, and 0 indicates no monotonic relationship. As shown by the green rectangles, the SCC values for Zero4 and Pole4 are near −1 across all 12 batteries, indicating a consistent and almost perfect negative monotonic relationship, demonstrating the potential of Zero4 and Pole4 for capacity estimation. This is also consistent with Fig. 6, where the main EIS variation (indicated by the arrows) happens within the 1 Hz to 100 Hz frequency range, approximately, corresponding to the frequency of Zero4 and Pole4. For the other zeros and poles, the SCC exhibits more variation across different batteries, with lower absolute values and inconsistent signs. This suggests their weaker and less stable correlations with capacity, making them less reliable for capacity estimation. Thus, Zero4 and Pole4 are selected as potential capacity estimation features.

Based on the SCC analysis results, an ablation study is conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of Pole4, Zero4, and their combination (Pole4＋Zero4) for capacity estimation. Three models are tested, including Polynomial, SVM, and GPR, with model parameters summarized in Table 2. Model training and testing are performed on batteries 25C01, 25C05, 35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02, with the LOOCV method, where each battery is iteratively used as the test set while the others serve as the training set. The RMSPE results are shown in Fig. 10. It can be observed that for the Polynomial model in Fig. 10(a), Pole4 outperforms Zero4 and Pole4＋Zero4 in 5 out of 6 batteries, while for the SVM and GPR models shown in Figs. 10(b) and (c), Pole4 performs better in 4 out of 6 batteries. For all three tested models, the Pole4 feature shows the lowest mean RMSPE, indicating the higher accuracy of the Pole4 feature for capacity estimation. Besides, the pole feature has better interpretability compared to zeros. As shown in the 2-RC ECM transfer function (6), (7), and (8), poles can be directly derived as
− 1 /
R
1
C
1
and
− 1 /
R
2
C
2
, with each pole corresponding to a parallel RC module, and the pole can be associated with the internal battery process through pole frequency. In contrast, the zeros are a couple of different RC modules and
R
0
, resulting in more complex behavior and physical meaning as the battery ages. Thus, considering the higher capacity estimation accuracy and better interpretability, Pole4 alone is employed as a feature for capacity estimation.

Moreover, compared to single-frequency-based features, the pole feature is more robust. Figs. 9(b) and (c) show the SCC between the impedance of the Real part,
Z
Re
, and the imaginary part,
Z
Im
, with capacity across different frequencies. In Fig. 9(c), it can be observed in the green rectangular region that
Z
Im
at many frequencies between approximately 1 Hz and 20 Hz exhibit consistent and high absolute value of SCC with capacity, offering many options for feature selection. On the dataset used in this study, different researchers have identified distinct feature frequencies. For example, research [19] selected
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz as the most relevant feature using correlation and feature consistency analysis, while study [18] identified
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz and 233.87 Hz for 25C01, 25C02, 25C03 and 25C04 using ARD method, but only 17.8 Hz for 35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02. Additionally, when applying the ARD method to 25C05, 25C06, 25C07, and 25C08, 6.98 Hz became the most significant feature. This indicates that the selection of feature frequency strongly depends on the battery, temperature, and feature selection method. Although the selected frequencies in these studies are reasonable, the capacity estimation performance varies significantly depending on the chosen frequency. As shown in Figs. 11(a) and (b), the relationship between
Z
Im
and capacity at 2.16 Hz and 17.8 Hz is significantly different. Fig. 11(c) presents the
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz and 17.8 Hz for 25C01 and 25C05 to provide a typical case. It can be observed that the relationship between
Z
Im
and capacity at 2.16 Hz of the two batteries is relatively consistent but diverges notably at 17.8 Hz, indicating that for the single-frequency-based method, the selected frequency will affect capacity estimation performance significantly.

In contrast, the system pole inherently captures the system dynamics over a range, as the influence of a pole typically extends over one order of magnitude around its frequency [32]. For example, Pole4 in Table 1 with a frequency of 38.27 Hz mainly captures system dynamics within a frequency range of 3.83 Hz to 382.7 Hz. This indicates that poles are less dependent on a single frequency, thus more robust to the frequency level disturbances. Besides, temperature also affects EIS. For example, as shown in Fig. 11(b), the relationship between
Z
Im
and capacity at 17.8 Hz of 25 °C batteries differs a lot from batteries cycled under 35 °C and 45 °C. However, the proposed pole feature is observed to be less sensitive to temperature variation and demonstrates higher capacity estimation performance and lower performance variation across different batteries cycled under different temperatures. Further discussions on this point are provided in Section 3.6.

On the dataset used in this research, significant battery inconsistencies are observed, particularly batteries cycled at 25 °C. This section discusses these inconsistencies and classifies the 12 batteries into two groups for the following research in this paper, based on capacity and impedance behavior.

The most notable inconsistency is the capacity degradation rates. Fig. 12(a) shows the number of cycles required for the 12 batteries to reach 70% maximum capacity. In the dataset, all data are recorded after 30 initialization cycles, but for some batteries, degradation to 70% occurs before the first recorded cycle. These batteries are shown as 30 in Fig. 12(a). It can be seen that all batteries cycled at 45 °C take over 500 cycles before reaching 70% capacity, 35 °C batteries take approximately 300 cycles, while batteries cycled at 25 °C take significantly fewer cycles. This may be attributed to slower lithium–ion diffusion at lower temperatures, leading to a reduction in battery performance. However, at 25 °C, batteries 25C01 and 25C05 demonstrated much longer lifespans, taking 168 and 110 cycles, respectively, to reach 70% capacity, whereas some batteries cycled at 25 °C degraded within fewer than 30 cycles, with almost one-sixth variation, indicating the significant battery inconsistencies among 25 °C batteries.

Study [21], which uses the same dataset, also claims the battery inconsistency and identifies battery 25C04, 25C07, and 25C08 as outlier data. Study [19] also discovers the battery inconsistency and attributes it to battery damage under high charge rates. Their study employs historical cycling data to perform feature normalization to reduce inconsistencies. However, considering the significantly different degradation rates of 25C01 and 25C05 discussed above, their aging mechanisms may differ from other 25 °C batteries. Therefore, the validity of analyzing all 25 °C batteries together warrants careful reconsideration. In this study, the 12 batteries are divided into two groups: Group 1 includes the outlier 25 °C batteries (25C01 and 25C05) along with the 35 °C and 45 °C batteries (35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02), Group 2 consists of the remaining batteries (25C02, 25C03, 25C04, 25C06, 25C07, and 25C08). Supporting evidence for this classification is provided below.

First, in Fig. 12(a), batteries 25C01 and 25C05 are identified as outliers within the 25 °C group using the interquartile range (IQR) method, indicating their difference from the other 25 °C batteries. The slower capacity degradation rate of these two batteries is more like those cycled at 35 °C and 45 °C. In addition, as shown in Fig. 12(b), batteries 25C01, 25C05, 35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02 (Group 1, denoted by dots) have very similar distribution on the Pole4 versus capacity plane, which are consistently distributed around a curve (green line). In contrast, batteries 25C02, 25C03, 25C04, 25C06, 25C07, and 25C08 (Group 2, denoted by triangles) deviate from this curve and exhibit a more scattered distribution. Beyond Pole4, differences between the two groups are also evident in other poles and zeros. A representative case is Pole2 behavior as shown in Fig. 12(c), where Group 1 (dots) and Group 2 (triangles) occupy entirely separate regions on Pole2 versus capacity plane, indicating battery dynamic difference between the two groups. This characteristic of Pole2 makes it a practical indicator for evaluating whether a battery will exhibit long cycle life or rapid capacity degradation. Further evidence can also be found in the raw EIS data. In Fig. 9(b), the SCC between
Z
Re
and the capacity of 25C01 and 25C05 is close to −1 across most frequencies, which are more like 35 °C and 45 °C batteries, rather than other 25 °C batteries (Group 2) that have SCC close to 1 at most frequencies. Overall, comprehensive results suggest that batteries 25C01 and 25C05 may have similar dynamic characteristics with the 35 °C and 45 °C batteries and are different from other 25 °C batteries. The similar dynamic characteristics potentially suggest similar degradation patterns. Therefore, batteries 25C01, 25C05 are grouped with 35 °C and 45 °C batteries (35C01, 35C02, 45C01, and 45C02) as Group 1, and the other 25 °C batteries (25C02, 25C03, 25C04, 25C06, 25C07, and 25C08) are classified as Group 2.

A potential reason for the rapid capacity degradation observed in Group 2 batteries compared to Group 1 is damage induced by high-rate charging, which is also the reason given by research [19]. The most common damage due to high charging rates is lithium plating, which consumes active lithium ions and electrolyte, leading to accelerated capacity loss [38]. Plating is more likely to occur at high charging rates under lower temperatures [39], which may explain why all Group 2 batteries that exhibit rapid degradation are cycled at 25 °C. Furthermore, lithium plating may damage the SEI layer, influencing the battery dynamics behavior [38], consistent with discrepancies observed between the two groups in the raw EIS data, Pole4, and Pole2, as discussed above. Lithium plating may also lead to more disordered and unpredictable SEI layer growth [38], which is consistent with Fig. 12(b), where Group 1 batteries are relatively concentrated around a single trend, while Group 2 batteries exhibit greater variability. It is worth noting that the above explanation is one of the possible reasons with reference to the previous research [19] and the battery operation conditions, and future work could involve more battery samples and detailed characterization of SEI growth to further investigate the battery inconsistencies.

Based on the analysis above, capacity estimation is performed separately for the two battery groups. In each group, the LOOCV method, where each battery is iteratively used as the test set while the others serve as the training set, is employed to evaluate the feature effectiveness comprehensively. Pole4 is used as a feature and compared with some features of existing works. Three regression models, including polynomial, SVM, and GPR, are tested, and the model parameters are listed in Table 2.

Fig. 13 shows the capacity estimation results of the six batteries in Group 1. Each row represents a regression model, and each column represents a battery. In each subplot, the performance of the Pole4 feature is compared with some other features in existing studies:
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz [19] (identified through correlation and feature consistency analysis),
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz [18] (identified via ARD), and the charge transfer resistance (
R
ct
) [26] derived from the ECM method. It can be observed that the Pole4 feature demonstrates considerable capacity estimation performance for different temperatures and batteries, and the estimated capacity is close to the measured capacity, indicating the effectiveness and robustness to temperature and battery variation of the proposed feature. In contrast, for the single-frequency features (
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz and
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz), the estimated capacity shows a substantial difference to real capacity at both 25 °C and 35 °C compared to 45 °C. For the
R
ct
feature, the performance is relatively good under 45 °C and 35 °C, but the estimated value shows significant difference with the measurement at 25 °C.

To quantitatively compare the performance of different features, the RMSPE of capacity estimation for Group 1 and Group 2 batteries are calculated, as summarized in Tables 3 and 4. The tables show that the proposed Pole4 feature consistently has a lower mean and standard deviation of RMSPE than other features across all three regression models. For example, on Group 1 batteries, the polynomial model with the Pole4 feature has a mean RMSPE of 2.22%, compared to 4.69%, 5.27%, and 3.63% of the
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz feature [19],
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz feature [18], and the charge transfer resistance feature
R
ct
[26], respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of the Pole4 feature in capacity estimation. Moreover, as shown in the tables, the proposed Pole4 feature achieves consistently good capacity estimation accuracy across all temperature conditions, whereas other features, particularly the single-frequency features, the accuracy decreases at 25 °C, indicating that the proposed feature is more robust to temperature variation. This superiority is potentially due to the nature of the Pole4 feature, which captures the EIS behavior over a broader frequency range compared to single-frequency features. As a system-level characteristic rather than a frequency-level feature, the pole reflects the overall dynamic behavior of the system. While temperature changes affect impedance at multiple frequencies, the pole feature integrates these variations from a system-wide perspective. This integration may contribute to its reduced sensitivity to temperature.

In addition, as discussed in Section 3.4, single-frequency features are generally less robust to battery variation. As shown in Table 3, regardless of the regression model used, using
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz as a feature results in significantly lower accuracy in estimating the capacity of battery 25C01 compared to using
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz. However, for another battery at the same temperature, 25C05, using
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz outperforms the 2.16 Hz feature. For example, for battery 25C01, the Polynomial model RMSPE using
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz feature is 8.46%, while using
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz has an RMSPE of 17.41%, which is significantly higher. However, for battery 25C05, the Polynomial model RMSPE for the
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz feature is 5.58%, while
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz has an RMSPE of 3.61%, which is lower than the 2.16 Hz feature. This indicates that the effectiveness of using a particular frequency as a feature for capacity estimation varies considerably across different batteries. In other words, it may not be robust. In contrast, the Pole feature corresponds to the system dynamics across a frequency range and is less affected by single-frequency differences, thus offering more accurate and stable capacity estimation results.

Moreover, it can also be found that across all features and regression models, the capacity estimation performance for Group 1 batteries is better than Group 2 batteries, which can be attributed to two factors:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Inconsistencies between Class 1 and Class 2 batteries. As discussed in Section 3.5, Group 2 batteries may suffer from damage due to high-rate charging at lower temperatures, potentially leading to disordered SEI layer growth, which makes the degradation pattern more difficult to predict [40].

Significantly less data for Class 2 batteries due to rapid degradation. For instance, all Class 1 batteries recorded over 300 cycles of data, while Class 2 batteries, such as 25C04 and 25C08, recorded only 35 and 37 cycles, respectively. Less data may adversely affect the model training process and reliability.

ce:section.ce:para:
Overall, the proposed feature outperforms the single-frequency features and ECM-based features
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz [19] and
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz [18] and ECM-based feature
R
ct
[26] across three different regression models. The pole-based feature is directly extracted by fitting the EIS data using the ZPG model, without requiring accurate battery modeling or prior battery knowledge. Besides, compared to single-frequency features, the pole feature naturally captures EIS information in a frequency range and demonstrates both accuracy and robustness across different batteries and temperatures.

Due to the uncertainty in EIS measurements, the robustness to noise of capacity estimation methods based on EIS presents a challenge. This section conducts a noise robustness analysis on the Pole4 feature and compares it with some existing features:
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz [19],
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz [18], and the ECM-based charge transfer resistance feature
R
ct
[26], respectively. According to the law of error propagation:
(9)

where Z , U , and I represent the impedance, voltage, and current, respectively, Δ represents the error, adding noise to measured voltage U or current I is equivalent to adding noise to Z . Thus, a Gaussian zero-mean noise is added to the real and imaginary parts of the raw EIS data to simulate the measurement noise.

The measurement noise can be expressed as:
(10)

)
where
Z
noise
denotes the noise in the impedance measurement, and σ is the standard deviation of the Gaussian noise. According to the analysis in [19], the variance of EIS noise is set to 1% of the measured value at all frequencies. Based on the noisy EIS data, the Pole4 feature and
R
ct
feature are extracted by fitting a ZPG model and an ECM model, respectively, while the single-frequency based features,
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz and 17.8 Hz, are extracted from noisy EIS directly. Their capacity estimation performance is tested on polynomial, SVM and GPR models (as shown in Table 2) on Group 1 batteries, and compared with the unnoisy results as shown in Fig. 14.

Fig. 14 shows that under noisy conditions, the RMSPE of the Pole4 feature increases only slightly, while the RMSPE of other features shows substantial increases. For example, when using the Pole4 feature and the GPR model, the median RMSPE rises from 2.24% to 2.60%, with an increment of 0.36%. In contrast, when using the
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz feature and the GPR model, the median RMSPE rises from 3.87% to 6.97%, with an increment of 3.1%, indicating that the Pole4 feature is less sensitive to measurement noise. This could be attributed to the nature of the pole feature that captures EIS information over a frequency range, offering an averaging effect of multiple frequencies and is therefore less sensitive to noise. Besides, poles are extracted from the ZPG model, which is essentially a curve-fitting technique. Although noise can have a significant effect on EIS at some frequencies, its impact on the overall trend of EIS over frequency is limited, and its influence on the fitted curve and the pole feature is therefore also limited.

Overall, the pole feature demonstrates better noise robustness compared to single-frequency features and the ECM-based
R
ct
feature, offering a more reliable solution for capacity estimation under limited EIS measurement accuracy.

In practical applications, historical battery capacity data is sometimes available. In this case, capacity can be considered time series data, and each battery cycle can be regarded as a time step. Time series models, such as the ARX and LSTM models, can be used for capacity forecasting, which is crucial for predictive maintenance and replacement planning.

To analyze the effectiveness of the Pole4 feature in time series models and capacity forecasting, this section first employs past capacity values solely as model input to perform multiple cycle capacity forecasting, and then incorporates the Pole4 feature and compares it with some existing features to evaluate their impact on model performance. The multi-step forecasting technique is a typical application in time series models. A 30-cycle forecasting for a battery implies using historical capacity data from 30 cycles prior, along with current cycle inputs (such as EIS data), to predict the capacity of the present cycle. This approach is suitable for practical scenarios where EIS data is available but capacity measurements are taken irregularly. In cases without input data, only historical capacity values are used for forecasting.

Two classical time series models, the ARX model and the LSTM model, are tested in this section based on historical capacity data, evaluating capacity estimation performance both without input and with inputs including Pole4,
Z
Im
at 2.16 Hz [19],
Z
Im
at 17.8 Hz [18], and ECM-based charge transfer resistance
R
ct
[26]. The model parameters are shown in Table 5. Similar to the capacity estimation in Section 3.6, the capacity forecasting is also performed on the two battery groups separately. Each battery is employed as the training dataset and tested on the other batteries in the same group. Forecasting steps are defined as a percentage of the total recorded battery cycles to account for varying battery lifespans and degradation rates. For example, a 10% forecasting step corresponds to forecasting 70 cycles ahead for battery 25C01 (700 total cycles) and 62 cycles ahead for battery 45C02 (620 total cycles), ensuring consistent evaluation and comparability. In this research, the forecasting step is selected as 30% of the total battery cycles.

The RMSPE of capacity forecasting is shown in Fig. 15. The legend “None” indicates forecasting based solely on historical capacity data, while other legends represent forecasting using both capacity and the additional features. For Group 1 batteries, both ARX and LSTM models show a significant reduction in forecasting RMSPE when additional inputs are included. Adding Pole4 as an input yields the lowest error, with an RMSPE of 1.77% compared to 4.70% for the no-input condition, highlighting its effectiveness for time-series model capacity forecasting. Besides, as Group 1 includes batteries at 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C, this also demonstrates the effectiveness of the Pole4 feature across different batteries and temperatures. For Group 2 batteries, the forecasting performance of incorporating additional features is similar to forecasting performance using historical capacity alone. This may be due to the inherently low capacity estimation accuracy of EIS-based features in Group 2 (as discussed in Section 3.6), and the model relies more on historical capacity data. Furthermore, Group 2 forecasting shows many outliers and high RMSPE values, especially with the LSTM model. A potential reason is the lack of training data. For instance, batteries 25C04 and 25C08 have only 35 and 37 recorded cycles, respectively, compared to over 300 cycles for Group 1. A model with insufficient training data may fail to capture battery characteristics, leading to reduced performance.

Overall, incorporating the Pole4 feature into time series models significantly improves capacity forecasting performance compared to employing solely historical capacity data or the frequency-based features and ECM-based
R
ct
feature, indicating the effectiveness of the Pole4 feature in time series model forecasting, providing a potential solution for battery health prediction and predictive maintenance.

This study introduces a novel and interpretable feature for lithium–ion battery capacity estimation and forecasting by fitting EIS data using the ZPG model. The key Pole4 feature, which is extracted from the ZPG model, captures system dynamics over a frequency range rather than relying on a single EIS point, and is shown to be robust across different batteries and temperature conditions. Unlike features from ECM-based methods, the ZPG model does not require prior battery knowledge or circuit design, offering generalizability and ease of implementation. Compared with classical features, including single-frequency impedance values and ECM-derived parameters, Pole4 consistently achieves lower RMSPE values in capacity estimation tasks using the polynomial, SVM, and GPR models, and improves the performance of ARX and LSTM models in multi-cycle-ahead forecasting. Moreover, noise robustness tests indicate that Pole4 is less sensitive to measurement disturbances due to its frequency-averaging nature. The comprehensive result demonstrates the potential of the ZPG model pole feature as a reliable and physically relevant indicator of battery capacity, providing a foundation for more accurate and interpretable battery management solutions.
Chenyi Li: Visualization, Software, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Long Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
